title: The $199 Palm Pre that's Really $299 for Some
author: Zoli Erdos, CloudAve
abstract: CloudAve blog post (May 19 2009, Zoli Erdos) criticizing Sprint/Palm's pricing of the upcoming Palm Pre smartphone: advertised at $199 with qualifying data plan but requiring a $100 mail-in rebate, so effective immediate price $299. Erdos cites BusinessWeek's 2005 'Great Rebate Runaround' report by Brian Grow, where 'Peter S. Kastner, a director of consulting firm Vericours Inc.' estimated 'fully 40% of all rebates never get redeemed,' translating to $2B+ extra revenue annually for retailers and suppliers. Erdos adds a personal Handspring Treo rebate horror story and argues for '$299 transparency' over deceptive rebate structures. This is syndication of a 2005 BusinessWeek Kastner/Vericours quote, wrapped in 2009 Palm Pre commentary by Erdos.
importance_rationale: Documents continued industry use of Kastner's 40% breakage statistic 4 years after original BusinessWeek 2005 publication, applied to a major smartphone launch; captures the specific Vericours affiliation during Kastner's post-Aberdeen consulting period (2005-2008).
relevance_rationale: Smartphone subsidies/rebates evolved into carrier-contract device pricing and then monthly-device-payment plans (2015+); rebate-specific tactics largely phased out.
prescience_rationale: Erdos' call for $299 transparency anticipated the industry shift — US carriers largely abandoned mail-in rebates on smartphones by 2013, replaced with installment plans and contract-tied subsidies (2013-2018) and later financing programs (Apple, Samsung, Google direct). Palm Pre itself sold poorly; Palm was acquired by HP in 2010 ($1.2B); webOS was discontinued 2011-2012.
